summary: [insight] Stepan Company stands alone in this single-customer Chemicals cohort yet exhibits a critical architectural anti-pattern: despite contracting a comprehensive SAP stack (BTP, BDC, Ariba, Concur, SuccessFactors), the Integration Suite spine is eroding 17% while Business Data Cloud sits at $0 consumed, meaning this specialty chemicals manufacturer is operating disconnected point solutions rather than the integrated enterprise architecture required for regulated operations, spend governance, and the margin discipline essential in commodity-sensitive chemical markets.

[insight] Stepan's consumption pattern reveals a chemicals-industry-relevant failure mode: peripheral advisory layers (Preferred Success at 118%, Concur Analytics over-consuming) are well-funded while every system-of-record foundation (Employee Central contracting, Ariba Catalog/Sourcing collapsing, BDC dormant) decays — for a manufacturer subject to EPA/OSHA compliance and raw material cost volatility, this inverted spending profile leaves Stepan without the unified data posture needed for regulatory audit trails, procurement cost control, or AI-enabled demand forecasting.

[insight] Stepan's $7,487 dormant Business Data Cloud investment represents the largest untapped consumption opportunity in this Chemicals portfolio — activating BDC as the cross-domain analytics layer connecting Ariba spend, Concur expense, and SuccessFactors workforce data would not only convert idle ACV but establish the AI-ready data foundation that chemicals companies require to operationalize Joule scenarios for formulation optimization, batch cost analysis, and workforce safety compliance.

[action] VAT should convene an enterprise integration architecture review with Stepan anchored on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, mapping the declining Integration Suite flows directly to broken Ariba catalog-to-invoice chains, Concur expense submission collapse, and dormant SuccessFactors-EC integrations — treating Integration Suite remediation as the single lever to arrest synchronized decay across all three domains and unlock the 76% budget-to-consumed gap.

[action] VAT should launch a Business Data Cloud activation workshop at Stepan that lands Employee Central, Ariba spend, and Concur expense as the first data products, converting the $7,487 dormant investment into a chemicals-relevant analytics backbone that proves cross-domain value through procurement spend visibility and workforce cost analytics before layering Joule AI scenarios.

[action] VAT should redirect Stepan's over-consuming Preferred Success SuccessFactors entitlements into a clean-core governance program pairing HCM re-engagement with BTP extensibility review, ensuring the HXM module decay and unused Joule Studio/Technology Foundation capacity are addressed as one architectural intervention — critical for a chemicals manufacturer needing integrated EHS compliance and workforce planning capabilities.

[insight] BTP platform activation is the shared architectural gap across all three customers — Domino's shows dormant Integration Suite and SAP Build, Kraft Heinz has Finance BTP Enterprise Agreement at $0 while compute overheats at 148%, and S.C. Johnson has near-zero CPEA consumption across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance — meaning this $6.5M ACV Consumer Products cohort has collectively paid for the connective fabric required for AI-ready, demand-driven CPG operations but is running disconnected transactional islands instead, blocking the margin recovery and supply chain responsiveness that justify the investment.

[insight] Kraft Heinz leads this cohort on transactional execution maturity — Ariba Buying, S/4HANA Finance, BTP runtime, and Signavio Process Intelligence all run ahead of plan — but trails Domino's and S.C. Johnson on governance and planning layer activation, with Sourcing, Supplier Risk, Spend Analysis, SAC BI, and Process Management sitting dormant against a ~$3M contracted envelope, making Kraft Heinz the peer best positioned to close insight-to-action loops if upstream analytics are activated.

[insight] S.C. Johnson is the cohort laggard on end-to-end product lifecycle integration — SAP Integrated Product Development sits at 5.7% YTD with two zero-consumption months while Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration dropped 28% in May — whereas Domino's maintains active Business Network transactional flows and Kraft Heinz has a $2.19M Supply Chain Collaboration ceiling to activate, leaving S.C. Johnson uniquely exposed on the CPG-critical capability of launching new products and executing resilient supply against retailer service-level commitments.

[action] Run a cohort-wide BTP activation sprint with a shared playbook for Domino's (Integration Suite iFlows for IBP-to-Business Network and Ariba Sourcing-to-Buying), Kraft Heinz (Finance BTP Enterprise Agreement first workload plus Integration Suite reactivation for Ariba–S/4HANA–IBP–Signavio–SAC flows), and S.C. Johnson (CPEA workloads integrating Concur, Blackline, and BDC into S/4HANA), converting the portfolio's dominant shelfware pattern into governed cross-domain connectivity.

[action] Prioritize Kraft Heinz for an Insight-to-Action activation engagement that pairs each dormant governance and analytics asset (SAC BI, Signavio Process Management, Ariba Spend Analysis, Supplier Risk) with its over-consuming transactional counterpart and named business owners, using Kraft Heinz's transactional maturity leadership as the foundation for a reference architecture the other two customers can follow.

[action] Conduct a product-to-supply-chain lifecycle recovery review at S.C. Johnson anchoring SAP Integrated Product Development on a single PLM-to-S/4HANA handover workflow paired with a Business Network trading partner expansion sprint, addressing the cohort's most acute gap in CPG innovation-and-collaboration capability.

[insight] Abbott leads this life sciences cohort on integration fabric activation — Integration Suite is running at 107% while Eli Lilly's Integration Suite contracted by 54% — but both companies share the same structural failure: neither has connected their integration layer to an AI-ready analytics foundation, leaving Abbott's HANA Cloud vector engine dormant and Lilly's Joule agents scaling on a collapsing integration substrate, which in pharma means neither can deliver the governed, closed-loop batch-to-release and plan-to-pay processes that regulators and CFOs demand.

[insight] Eli Lilly is behind Abbott on supply chain planning activation — Lilly's IBP and Batch Release Hub remain dormant while Abbott has at least deployed IBP modules (though underutilized) — meaning Lilly's $191K Batch Release Hub and $209K IBP gap represents a complete absence of GxP-compliant release automation that Abbott has structurally positioned itself to achieve, a critical miss for a pharma company where batch disposition velocity directly impacts revenue recognition and patient access.

[insight] Both customers exhibit the same anti-pattern of advisory over-consumption without platform activation — Abbott's Preferred Success Ariba burns at 120% while Ariba transactional adoption sits at 21-68%, and Lilly's Preferred Success entitlements across Signavio, Commerce, and SuccessFactors burn at flat rates while SAC Planning (4.2%), Commerce, and Batch Release Hub remain dormant — indicating both enterprises are funding guidance they cannot operationalize, a systemic governance failure that puts $1.5M+ combined ACV at risk of delivering zero transformation value.

[action] Convene a joint Abbott-Lilly pharma value-chain architecture summit focused on the shared gap of connecting integration fabric to AI-ready analytics, using Abbott's active Integration Suite as a reference pattern and Lilly's Joule investment as the target activation layer, with explicit deliverables mapping batch-to-release, plan-to-pay, and demand-to-supply closed loops onto BTP and HANA Cloud.

[action] Stand up a cohort-wide Value Realisation Office model — piloting at Lilly where the advisory-to-activation disconnect is most severe ($913K SAC Planning gap, $191K Batch Release Hub gap) — that consolidates all Preferred Success entitlements under a single adoption backlog with measurable platform activation milestones replacing flat entitlement burn, then extend the governance model to Abbott.

[action] Run an IBP-to-Batch Release Hub integration sprint at Lilly to close the GxP automation gap that Abbott has structurally addressed, positioning Lilly's dormant Batch Release Hub as the downstream consumer of IBP supply signals and establishing the make-release-report process flow that underpins pharma revenue recognition and regulatory compliance.

[insight] Marathon Petroleum Company LP stands alone in this single-customer cohort but exhibits a dangerous architectural anti-pattern for a downstream refiner: transactional execution layers (Ariba Buying at 138%, Signavio Process Management at 164%, Joule Studio at 660%) are scaling without corresponding governance instrumentation (Ariba Sourcing/Catalog, Process Intelligence, Preferred Success all flat or declining), creating a spend-without-control posture that directly erodes EBITDA in an industry where feedstock procurement discipline and turnaround process assurance determine margin capture.

[insight] Marathon's Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement represents the largest dormant strategic asset in this portfolio — Database Cloud and Joule Studio consumption is exploding while BDC Public, Technology Foundation, and Finance allocations sit fully unrealized, meaning Marathon is funding an enterprise-grade BTP platform but operating it as fragmented project sandboxes rather than the unified data-and-integration fabric required to industrialize AI-driven yield optimization and closed-loop hydrocarbon accounting across refinery operations.

[insight] Marathon's S/4HANA Private Cloud core is running unprotected — zero Preferred Success advisory consumption combined with no activated BTP extensibility plane means every Joule, Signavio, and Ariba scaling event lands directly on-core, compounding technical debt that will crystallize as upgrade blockers at the next S/4HANA release boundary and jeopardize the clean-core posture essential for regulatory reporting agility in a hydrocarbon operation subject to EPA, OSHA, and SEC climate disclosure mandates.

[action] Convene an enterprise BTP consolidation workshop for Marathon that unifies the fragmented Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement entitlements (~$118K Database Cloud, ~$190K Joule Studio, plus dormant BDC Public, Technology Foundation, and Finance pools) into a governed consumption plan with workload-to-domain traceability, unlocking the integrated data fabric required for refinery-wide AI and planning use cases.

[action] Execute a closed-loop governance sprint for Marathon spanning Ariba Sourcing/Catalog and Signavio Process Intelligence (~$150K combined at-risk) to re-anchor these dormant capabilities to the overheating Buying/Invoicing and Process Management layers, restoring the sourcing-to-pay and model-to-measure feedback loops that convert procurement volume into auditable margin and compliance outcomes.

[action] Schedule a clean-core roadmap session for Marathon that re-activates the ~$34K unrealized Preferred Success entitlement and binds it to a BTP extensibility blueprint, ensuring Joule, Signavio, and Ariba extensions land off-core and positioning Marathon for frictionless S/4HANA upgrades critical to maintaining regulatory reporting agility.

[insight] Border States Industries stands as the sole customer in this wholesale distribution cohort, yet exhibits a structural misallocation pattern that positions it as architecturally constrained rather than immature — with BTP at 113.7% and Joule at 200% consumption against Commerce Cloud at 32% and SuccessFactors talent modules at 0-44%, the company has over-indexed on AI and integration runtime while leaving the B2B commerce and workforce productivity capabilities that define distributor competitiveness largely dormant.

[insight] Border States' platform ceiling risk is acute for wholesale distribution specifically — BTP serves as the integration backbone connecting Commerce Cloud storefronts to inventory availability, SuccessFactors to branch workforce scheduling, and Concur to job-costed expense management, meaning the 113.7% consumption cap directly throttles the connected distributor journeys (quote-to-cash, hire-to-deploy, procure-to-pay) that drive margin in a low-margin, high-velocity sector.

[insight] The ~$120K of contracted but dormant capacity in Commerce Cloud and SuccessFactors talent modules represents stranded investment in capabilities wholesale distributors need most — digital self-service for contractor and MRO buyers, rapid onboarding for seasonal warehouse staff, and compensation structures tied to branch performance — while the overage pressure sits on the very platform ($37K BTP, $19.5K Concur) required to activate those capabilities.

[action] Facilitate a cross-LoB entitlement rebalancing workshop for Border States to redirect the ~$120K in unused Commerce Cloud and SuccessFactors talent budget toward funding the BTP and Concur overages, converting dormant contract value into activated distributor capability without requiring net-new spend approval.

[action] Conduct a BTP architecture sizing review for Border States that models the combined integration load from Joule Studio, SAP Integration Suite, Commerce Cloud order flows, and SuccessFactors/Concur data synchronization to right-size the LPR868 agreement before the platform ceiling blocks AI and clean-core extensibility initiatives.

[action] Initiate a distributor journey activation assessment for Border States mapping the broken handoffs between Employee Central, Compensation, Concur Expense, and Commerce Cloud to identify which integration gaps are preventing the end-to-end B2B buyer and workforce journeys that the contracted suites were designed to deliver.

enterprise_architecture_insights: [insight] Abbott has contracted a complete SAP enterprise stack — HANA Cloud as data fabric, Integration Suite as integration backbone, Build as extensibility layer, and IBP/Ariba/C4C as operational systems — but the foundational platform tier (HANA Cloud at 65%, Build at 44%) is under-consumed while the operational tier is either ceiling-bound or flatlined, meaning Abbott is paying for a transformation-grade architecture while operating it as a collection of disconnected workloads worth roughly $3M+ in annual contract value at risk of delivering only run-rate value.

[insight] The closed-loop intelligence story across Abbott's enterprise is structurally broken in the same place every time — Integration Suite is overrun at 107% moving data between systems, but HANA Cloud's vector engine and data lake are dormant, SAP Build is idle, and SAC Planning is finance-only, so demand signals from IBP, pipeline from C4C, and spend from Ariba cannot converge into the AI-ready, plan-to-execute fabric that life sciences competitors are using to compress forecast-to-supply and commercial-to-cash cycles.

[insight] Abbott's adoption pattern shows a consistent inversion across every L1 — advisory and integration layers are over-consumed (Preferred Success Ariba 120%, Integration Suite 107%) while the transactional and analytical surfaces they are meant to activate are underperforming (Ariba suite 21–68%, C4C 58%, HANA Cloud 65%, IBP modules dormant), indicating enablement spend is funding guidance Abbott is not operationalizing and putting a combined ~$500K+ YTD shortfall against contracted outcomes.

[action] Convene a cross-domain Enterprise Architecture review anchored on HANA Cloud as the shared data foundation, with mandated workload onboarding from IBP (demand sensing), Ariba (supplier risk analytics), C4C (pipeline), and SAC Planning (workforce/capex) to convert the dormant $63K data backbone into the single source feeding every LoB.

[action] Redirect Preferred Success Ariba's over-consumed advisory entitlement and stand up a joint SAP Build + Integration Suite Center of Excellence chartered to compose the existing integration fabric into low-code apps for Ariba Buying, C4C mobile sales, and IBP collaborative response, closing the Build $935 gap and unlocking the underutilized Ariba/C4C $251K combined shortfall.

[action] Run a plan-to-execute integration coherence workshop tying SAC Planning, IBP, Ariba, and C4C onto a unified planning and execution model on HANA Cloud, with explicit scope to recover the $208K SAC Planning and $70K C4C shortfalls by extending planning into workforce, capex, and commercial pipeline domains rather than deepening finance-only use.

solutions_l1.name: BAIP - Data & AI
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Abbott's Data & AI foundation is architecturally inverted — HANA Cloud, the mission-critical data fabric meant to anchor every analytics and AI workload, is running at 65% attainment with $63K of contracted capacity at risk, while the consuming layers (SAC BI, Data Intelligence Cloud) are pinned at their ceilings, meaning the LoB is over-utilizing the visualization and orchestration surface on top of an under-utilized data backbone.

[insight] The analytics-to-AI value chain is operating as a reporting pipeline rather than an intelligence platform — Data Intelligence Cloud is healthy at 99% on pipelines and SAC BI is ceiling-bound on dashboards, but HANA Cloud's vector engine, data lake, and AI-ready data products are dormant, so Abbott is moving and visualizing data without activating the agentic AI and business data fabric capabilities the architecture was designed to deliver.

[action] Launch a HANA Cloud workload migration with Abbott's data platform team to onboard additional datamarts, vector engine, and data lake scenarios that close the $23,659 YTD gap and convert dormant capacity into the AI-ready foundation feeding SAC BI and Data Intelligence Cloud.

[action] Convert SAC BI's ceiling-bound reporting footprint into strategic adoption by scoping planning, predictive, and Joule-driven embedded analytics use cases at Abbott, and pair them with new Data Intelligence Cloud pipelines to absorb the recurring ~$718/month ACV headroom.

contract_insights: [insight] Five months of reported consumption total $9,049 against a budget of $9,128 (99.1% attainment) — Data Intelligence Cloud is tracking on plan, indicating the pipeline and data orchestration workloads are operationally embedded.

[insight] Monthly consumption oscillates between $1,528 (Feb) and $2,008 (Jan) against a steady ACV ceiling of $2,528 — the $1,000 ACV gap in February shows headroom exists to expand data pipeline scope without breaching contract limits.

[action] Lock in Data Intelligence Cloud's on-pace trajectory by formalizing the active use cases driving the $9,049 YTD consumption and replicating them across adjacent Abbott business units to absorb the remaining $12,669 of annual budget.

[action] Close the recurring ACV-to-consumed gap (averaging ~$718/month) on Data Intelligence Cloud by scoping an additional data integration or ML pipeline workload that lifts monthly consumption closer to the $2,528 ACV ceiling.

[insight] Jan–Mar consumed $2,796 against a $2,486 monthly budget (112.5% attainment, -$311 gap each month) while Apr–May landed exactly at $2,486 — the overage normalization signals usage has plateaued at the contract ceiling, indicating SAC BI is anchored to a fixed reporting workload rather than expanding to new user groups.

[insight] Projected annual consumed of $32,065 against a $27,912 budget puts Abbott on track to overshoot by $4,153 (14.9%) — the overrun is real but concentrated in Q1, meaning without a usage governance conversation Abbott risks unbudgeted true-up exposure on SAC BI.

[action] Run a SAC BI content and user audit at Abbott to identify which dashboards and personas drove the Q1 112.5% attainment, and right-size license allocation to prevent recurring monthly overages.

[action] Launch an SAC BI expansion review with Abbott to convert the steady-state consumption pattern into net-new use cases (planning, predictive, embedded analytics) so the contract value reflects strategic adoption rather than ceiling-bound reporting.

[insight] Consumed $43,871 of $67,530 budgeted through May (65% attainment) with all five reported months landing between 59% and 72% — this is a consistent under-pacing pattern, not a one-off dip, indicating steady-state usage below the planned HANA Cloud workload footprint.

[insight] Cumulative budget gap has widened to $23,659 with 7 months and $101,096 of remaining budget to consume — at the current ~$8,774 monthly run rate, projected full-year consumption lands near $105K against a $168,626 ceiling, leaving roughly $63K of contracted value at risk of going unused.

[action] Run a HANA Cloud workload review with Abbott's data platform team to identify additional databases, datamarts, or analytics workloads that can be migrated onto existing HANA Cloud capacity to close the $23,659 year-to-date budget gap.

[action] Launch a HANA Cloud scale-up plan targeting the $9,310 May ACV gap — focus on enabling vector engine, data lake, or replication scenarios that lift monthly consumption above the $13,826 budget line rather than tracking 40% below it.

solutions_l1.name: BAIP - Technology Foundation
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] The integration backbone is load-bearing while the extensibility layer is idle — SAP Integration Suite is running hot at 107%+ attainment every month (projected $44K annual overage on a $282K budget) because Abbott has standardized on it as the mission-critical API and process integration fabric, yet SAP Build, the companion low-code extension layer designed to surface those integrated processes as apps and workflows, is plateaued at ~$120/month and tracking to only 44% of its $3,352 annual budget.

[insight] Abbott has architecturally contracted for a complete BTP foundation but is operationally running only half of it — Integration Suite is delivering the 'integrate AI agents, apps, and data' capability at scale, while SAP Build's low-code app, workflow automation, and citizen-developer capabilities remain confined to a fixed developer pocket, meaning integrated data and APIs are available but not being composed into new business-facing applications or automations.

[action] Right-size the SAP Integration Suite contract against the $44K projected overage and pair the true-up with a message-packaging and reuse governance review with Abbott's integration leads to convert unplanned consumption into committed, governed capacity.

[action] Launch a SAP Build Center of Excellence and use-case discovery workshop at Abbott that explicitly targets extension scenarios on top of the existing Integration Suite APIs, closing the $935 YTD budget gap by converting integrated processes into low-code apps and automated workflows.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption has declined from $144 in Jan to $115 in May with attainment stuck between 34-46% — this flat-to-downward trend indicates SAP Build is being used by a fixed pocket of developers rather than expanding across new use cases or business units at Abbott.

[insight] Year-to-date consumed is $611 against a YTD budget of $1,546, leaving a $935 budget gap five months in and tracking toward only ~44% of the $3,352 annual budget — without an adoption intervention, more than half the contracted value will go unrealized.

[action] Run a SAP Build use-case discovery workshop with Abbott application and business process owners to identify 3-5 net-new low-code/process automation candidates that can close the $935 YTD budget gap.

[action] Establish a SAP Build Center of Excellence enablement track at Abbott to scale beyond the current stagnant user base and lift monthly consumption above the ~$120 plateau toward the $279 average monthly budget.

[insight] Consumption has exceeded budget every reported month (Jan–May all at or above 100%, peaking at 112.7% in May), and projected annual consumption of $326,567 overshoots the $282,226 budget by ~$44K — this is a sustained over-consumption pattern, not a one-time spike, signaling Integration Suite is mission-critical and the contract is undersized for actual demand.

[insight] May's budget gap widened to -$2,798 (112.7% attainment) versus a flat -$1,973 in Jan–Mar, showing consumption velocity is accelerating rather than stabilizing — without intervention the customer will burn through ACV headroom well before fiscal year end and face overage exposure.

[action] Initiate a commercial true-up conversation on SAP Integration Suite to right-size the contract against the $44K projected annual overage and convert unplanned overconsumption into committed capacity.

[action] Run an Integration Suite consumption review with Abbott's integration leads to identify which interfaces or message volumes are driving the consistent 107%+ attainment and govern usage through message packaging, reuse patterns, or tiering before overage compounds.

solutions_l1.name: Customer Experience
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Abbott's Customer Experience architecture is split between an active incentive engine and a stalled engagement front-end — SAP Incentive Management is running at a healthy ~$52K/month steady state driving commission accuracy and sales behavior, while Cloud for Customer has flatlined at $7–8K/month, meaning sales reps are being paid through SAP but not selling through it, breaking the CRM-to-compensation feedback loop.

[insight] Cloud for Customer's 58.4% YTD attainment and projected $70K shortfall against the $166K annual contract signal that core CX capabilities — opportunity management, service ticketing, and mobile CRM — are deployed but dormant, leaving Abbott with Incentive Management calculating payouts on sales data that originates outside the SAP customer engagement layer.

[action] Expand Cloud for Customer adoption by onboarding additional Abbott sales and service business units into opportunity management, service ticketing, and mobile CRM modules to close the $70K projected consumption gap and feed clean pipeline data into Incentive Management.

[action] Reconcile the -$51,754 May entry on SAP Incentive Management with billing operations to confirm whether it is a true-up, credit memo, or data error, and restate May attainment to protect the integrity of the $632,962 annual view.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption has climbed only from $7,245 in Jan to $8,376 in May — a $1,131 increase over five months — showing Cloud for Customer usage is essentially flat, indicating Abbott has stabilized on a narrow set of users or use cases rather than expanding adoption.

[insight] Cumulative consumption of $39,889 against a year-to-date budget of $68,248 puts attainment at 58.4%, and at the current ~$8K/month run rate Abbott will land near $96K of the $166K annual contract — leaving roughly $70K of customer engagement value unrealized.

[action] Launch a Cloud for Customer expansion review with Abbott's sales operations team to identify untapped modules (sales planning, service ticketing, mobile CRM) that can absorb the $5K+ monthly budget gap.

[action] Convert the flat $7K–$8K monthly consumption pattern into a growth curve by enrolling additional Abbott business units onto Cloud for Customer through a structured onboarding sprint targeting the $70K projected shortfall.

[insight] May consumption posted at -$51,754 against a $52,747 budget, producing a -98.1% attainment — this negative figure indicates a credit, reversal, or billing correction rather than genuine usage, and it artificially masks the true adoption trajectory of SAP Incentive Management.

[insight] Jan–Apr consumption tracked tightly between 98.3% and 102.1% of budget, then May collapsed into negative territory — the four-month steady-state run rate of ~$52K/month shows healthy operational use of Incentive Management, but the May anomaly breaks the pattern and needs reconciliation before it distorts the $632,962 annual view.

[action] Investigate the -$51,754 May consumption entry on SAP Incentive Management with billing operations to confirm whether it reflects a true-up, credit memo, or data error, and restate the May attainment figure.

[action] Lock in the Jan–Apr ~$52.7K/month consumption cadence on SAP Incentive Management by validating that commission calculation cycles, payee counts, and plan rules remain stable, preventing the $252,534 remaining budget runway from drifting.

solutions_l1.name: Finance and Spend Management
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Abbott's Procure-to-Pay architecture is structurally underutilized despite being fully contracted — Ariba Sourcing (88.6% attainment, declining), Buying and Invoicing (61% attainment, flat), and Supplier Risk (21.3% attainment) are all operational but none are scaling, meaning sourcing events, requisitions, and risk monitoring are happening outside the platform and the integrated S2P value chain is not being realized.

[insight] The inverse adoption pattern between Preferred Success Ariba (120% over-consumption) and the underlying Ariba transactional suite (21–68% attainment) reveals that Abbott is consuming heavy advisory and enablement services without translating that guidance into expanded platform usage — the enablement motion is disconnected from the adoption outcome it is meant to drive.

[insight] SAC Planning is anchored in a narrow finance-core use case rather than functioning as the unified financial and operational planning layer it is architecturally designed to be — the 70.6% YTD attainment with no monthly breakout above 76% confirms planning models are not extending into workforce, capex, or operational domains, leaving cross-LoB planning capability dormant.

[action] Redirect Preferred Success Ariba advisory capacity toward activating dormant Supplier Risk coverage and reversing the Ariba Sourcing consumption decline, tying the over-consumed enablement entitlement to measurable expansion of the transactional Ariba footprint.

[action] Launch a P2P scope expansion initiative covering Ariba Buying and Invoicing catalog rollout, Sourcing event routing mandates, and Supplier Risk supplier-tier enrollment to close the combined ~$181K projected annual shortfall across the procurement stack.

[action] Activate workforce and capex planning models in SAP Analytics Cloud Planning to extend usage beyond the finance core and recover the $208,609 projected annual shortfall through new planner cohorts rather than deeper finance-only consumption.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumed $238,344 of $337,372 budgeted through May (70.6% attainment) with no month exceeding 76% — this consistent under-pacing reflects steady but shallow usage of SAP Analytics Cloud Planning, not a ramp-up curve.

[insight] May attainment dropped to 63.1% ($42,876 vs $67,970 budget), the second-lowest month of the year, reversing the modest recovery seen in March-April and signaling planning cycles are not expanding into new user groups or use cases.

[action] Launch a planner enablement sprint on SAP Analytics Cloud Planning targeting the $208,609 projected annual shortfall by activating workforce or capex planning models beyond the current finance core.
[action] Audit active SAC Planning users and story consumption against licensed capacity to identify why monthly consumption is stuck in the $43K–$51K band despite a $67K+ monthly budget.

[insight] Consumption across Jan–May has held in a tight $15K–$21K band against budgets averaging ~$29K, producing an attainment ceiling of 55–68% — this flat pattern indicates Ariba Buying and Invoicing is in steady-state use by a fixed user group rather than expanding across Abbott's procurement footprint.

[insight] Cumulative consumption of $89,733 against a cumulative budget of $147,453 leaves a $57,720 shortfall five months in, and with $215,357 projected against a $357,852 annual contract, Abbott is on track to leave ~$142K of Ariba Buying and Invoicing capacity unused.

[action] Launch a catalog and requisition expansion initiative for Ariba Buying and Invoicing targeting business units still transacting outside the platform to lift monthly consumption above the $29K budget line.

[action] Address the May dip to 55.8% attainment on Ariba Buying and Invoicing by auditing invoice automation and PO-flip enablement with AP, since invoice volume is the most direct lever to close the $26,259 ACV gap.

[insight] Consumption has declined from $9,566 in January to $8,549 in May with attainment slipping from 90.2% to 88.6% — this steady downward drift indicates Ariba Sourcing usage is losing momentum rather than scaling, suggesting sourcing events are not being consistently routed through the platform.

[insight] Projected annual consumed of $108,741 against a $117,373 budget leaves an $8,632 shortfall, and with cumulative YTD gaps of $8,788 against ACV already accrued, Abbott is on track to underutilize roughly 7% of contracted value unless sourcing event volume increases in the remaining 7 months.

[action] Run an Ariba Sourcing usage audit with Abbott's procurement leads to identify which sourcing categories and business units are bypassing the platform, targeting the $2,554 May ACV gap as the immediate recovery focus.

[action] Launch a sourcing event acceleration plan for Ariba Sourcing — including templated RFx enablement and category expansion — to reverse the four-month consumption decline and close the $8,632 projected annual budget shortfall.

[insight] Consumption climbed from $611 in March to $821 in May (a 34% increase over two months), but cumulative consumed of $3,570 against a YTD budget of $16,777 means Abbott has only attained 21.3% of planned pace — the upward trend is real but the slope is too shallow to recover the gap.

[insight] Projected annual consumption of $8,568 against a $39,545 budget puts Ariba Supplier Risk on track for ~21.7% utilization, leaving roughly $30,977 of contracted value unrealized — this is a value-realization risk, not just a consumption shortfall.
[action] Launch a Supplier Risk activation sprint with Abbott's procurement risk owners to expand supplier coverage and risk assessment cadence, directly targeting the $5,491 May ACV gap.

[action] Audit which Abbott business units and supplier tiers are currently enrolled in Ariba Supplier Risk monitoring versus contracted scope, and onboard the missing segments to close the $30,977 projected annual shortfall.

[insight] Consumed $139,251 against a budget of $116,051 through May — a $23,200 over-burn (120% attainment) indicates Abbott is using Preferred Success Ariba faster than planned and is on pace to exceed the $263,110 annual budget by ~$71,090.

[insight] Every reported month has run hot (attainment between 112% and 128%, never dipping to plan), showing this is sustained heavy engagement on Ariba enablement services rather than a one-time spike — demand for the entitlement is structurally underestimated.

[action] Trigger a mid-year true-up conversation on Preferred Success Ariba (LPR837) to address the projected $71,090 overage and right-size the entitlement before consumption exhausts the annual ceiling.

[action] Audit which Preferred Success Ariba engagements (CSM advisory, release guidance, expert sessions) are driving the 120%+ attainment and prioritize the highest-value workstreams so remaining capacity is reserved for Abbott's most strategic Ariba adoption outcomes.

solutions_l1.name: Supply Chain Management
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] IBP is architecturally positioned as a unified planning platform spanning demand sensing, inventory optimization, S&OP, and supply response, but Abbott's flat $123,616/month consumption pattern indicates only a narrow slice of this footprint is operational — the LoB is running steady-state S&OP and supply planning while the AI-powered demand prediction, inventory optimization, and collaborative response capabilities remain dormant, capping the planning transformation at a fraction of its intended value.

[insight] The lockstep precision of 100% monthly attainment against budget signals a mature, production-grade workload rather than experimental use, meaning Abbott has institutionalized IBP as a system of record for planning — but with $865,318 of remaining budget on a fixed trajectory and no module expansion, the LoB is on track to fully consume contract value without deepening architectural reach into demand-driven replenishment or scenario planning.

[action] Convene Abbott's supply chain planning leadership to scope activation of dormant IBP modules — specifically demand sensing, inventory optimization, and S&OP scenario planning — to convert the stable $1.48M annual run-rate into measurable forecast accuracy and working capital outcomes.

enterprise_architecture_insights: [insight] Domino's has contracted a coherent end-to-end digital backbone — HANA Cloud as the data foundation, Integration Suite as the integration spine, SAP Build for extensions, IBP feeding Business Network, and Ariba Sourcing feeding Ariba Buying — but every upstream node in each chain is contracting or flat while only the downstream transactional nodes (Buying, Concur, HANA storage) are active, meaning the enterprise is operating as a collection of disconnected systems of record rather than the connected, AI-ready operating model the contract envelope was sized for.

[insight] The BTP platform layer (HANA Cloud isolated from Datasphere/SAC, Integration Suite with no net-new iFlows, SAP Build contracting) is simultaneously under-adopted across data, integration, and extension dimensions, which structurally blocks the cross-domain stories Domino's most needs — closing the IBP-to-Business Network supplier loop, reconnecting Ariba Sourcing/Contracts to Buying, and surfacing store and ordering analytics — because every one of those flows depends on the same dormant BTP capabilities.

[action] Rebalance the Ariba footprint by launching a coordinated activation program that ties Ariba Sourcing event migration, Spend Analysis dashboard restoration for category managers, and Supplier Risk supplier onboarding to the existing Buying and Invoicing transaction flow, closing the combined ~$220K YTD upstream gap before truing up the $346K Buying overage.

[action] Stand up a Finance platform activation workstream that converts the May Profitability and Performance Management spike into scheduled monthly allocation and margin runs, activates underused S/4HANA Finance modules (Group Reporting, Predictive Accounting, Central Finance), and provisions a first BTP Enterprise Agreement workload to provide the integration backbone these finance capabilities depend on.

solutions_l1.name: Supply Chain Management

[insight] With only 20.8% YTD attainment against the $2.19M annual ceiling and a run rate trending toward ~$1.09M, the collaboration capabilities of forecast sharing, procurement collaboration, and supply resiliency are contracted but functionally dormant — the LoB is paying for network-wide visibility while operating with point-to-point supplier exchanges.

[action] Activate forecast collaboration and PO collaboration flows on Bus Netw Supply Chain Collaboration by integrating the network with S/4HANA and IBP for the top trading partners, converting onboarding waves into recurring transactional volume.

[action] Run a document-flow diagnostic on Bus Netw Supply Chain Collaboration to identify which POs, invoices, ASNs, and forecast signals remain off-network, and enable them in sequence to close the projected ~$1.09M shortfall against contracted capacity.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption jumped from $75K in March to $143K in April (a 91% surge) then dropped to $125K in May — this volatility on Bus Netw Supply Chain Collaboration indicates supplier onboarding waves rather than steady-state transactional flow, meaning adoption is project-driven and not yet embedded in daily procurement operations.

enterprise_architecture_insights: [insight] The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement appears as the connective tissue across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance — yet it shows runaway Database Cloud and Joule Studio consumption alongside fully dormant BDC Public, Technology Foundation, and Finance allocations, meaning Marathon is funding an enterprise BTP platform but operating it as disconnected project credits, which blocks the unified data-and-integration fabric needed to industrialize AI and closed-loop planning across the refinery and downstream value chain.

[insight] Marathon's transactional layers are overheating (Ariba Buying 138%, Signavio Process Management 164%, Joule Studio 660%, Database Cloud 537%) while their governance and measurement counterparts (Ariba Sourcing/Catalog, Process Intelligence, Preferred Success, SAC Planning) are flat or declining, indicating an enterprise pattern where execution is scaling without the upstream controls, analytics, and advisory scaffolding that convert volume into margin, compliance, and audit-ready outcomes — a critical risk for a hydrocarbon operator where spend discipline and process assurance directly drive EBITDA.

[insight] The S/4HANA Private Cloud core is running without its Preferred Success advisory layer and without an activated BTP extensibility plane underneath it, meaning every Joule, Signavio, and Ariba scaling event lands on a core that is neither guided for clean upgrades nor instrumented for side-by-side extension, compounding technical debt that will surface at the next S/4HANA release boundary.

[action] Convene an enterprise BTP architecture review to consolidate the fragmented Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement entitlements (~$118K Database Cloud run-rate, ~$190K Joule Studio run-rate, plus dormant BDC Public, Technology Foundation, and Finance pools) into a single governed consumption plan with workload-to-domain traceability.

[action] Run a closed-loop governance workshop spanning Finance & Spend Management and Business Transformation Management to re-anchor Ariba Sourcing/Catalog and Signavio Process Intelligence (~$150K at-risk) to the overheating Buying-and-Invoicing and Process Management layers, restoring the sourcing-to-pay and model-to-measure feedback loops.

[action] Schedule a Cloud ERP Private clean-core roadmap session that re-activates the ~$34K unrealized Preferred Success entitlement and binds it to a BTP extensibility blueprint so that Joule, Signavio, and Ariba extensions land off-core ahead of the next S/4HANA release cycle.
